does: This paper identifies and addresses the solve-to-judge gap in generative reward models (GRMs), where models can correctly solve problems but fail to judge solutions on 14%-37% of queries despite possessing the necessary solving capability. The authors propose S2J (Solve-to-Judge), a training approach that simultaneously leverages both solving and judging capabilities on a single GRM's output as supervision signals. By explicitly linking problem-solving and evaluation abilities during optimization, S2J reduces this capability gap. The method trains GRMs to recognize their own solving competence and transfer this understanding to judgment tasks, demonstrating improved reward modeling and evaluation performance across comprehensive experiments.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of generative reward models, which have become central to LLM alignment and preference learning following models like GPT-4 and Claude. It extends prior research on preference-based learning and reward modeling that typically treats judgment as a separate supervised learning task from problem-solving. The paper relates to broader work on capability alignment and behavioral consistency in LLMs, where models exhibiting strong capabilities in one domain may underperform in related domains. It also connects to research on self-awareness and introspective learning in language models, addressing the tension between what models can do and what they claim they can do, a phenomenon explored in recent work on model calibration and uncertainty estimation.
enables: This work opens directions for improving reward models' reliability in RLHF pipelines and alignment applications where judgment quality directly impacts model optimization. The solve-to-judge framework could be extended to other capability pairs where models possess latent abilities that don't transfer automatically to evaluation tasks. Future work might explore using solving demonstrations to provide richer supervision signals for other judgment tasks, or applying similar bridging techniques to other alignment problems where model capabilities remain dormant. The approach also enables development of more honest, self-aware reward models that better understand their own competencies, potentially improving feedback quality for iterative model improvement and reducing misalignment risks from overconfident or inaccurate evaluations.
